coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots enhance the recovery of microalgae bioresources from wastewater containing amoxicillin
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Zhenhao
Xue, Ren
Fu, Liang
Chen, Congli
Ndayisenga, Fabrice
Zhou, Dandan
coredata.dc:description: The cultivation of microalgae using wastewater could bring some major economic benefits; however, the toxics in wastewater typically lead to a reduction in bioresource production. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) could enhance the photosynthetic activity of Chlorella under antibiotic stress because they might optimize photoluminescence by red-shifting incident light. Adding of 1 mg/L CDs increased the specific growth rate of Chlorella by 36.0% (day 8–13) and 52.7% (day 14–18) and significantly increased photosystems II activity. This treatment also increased amoxicillin removal by 18.6%. Thus, the toxicity of residuals was significantly eliminated (P < 0.05). The removal of nitrogen and phosphorous was increased by 14.6% and 9.9%, respectively. The production of pigments, lipids and proteins was increased by 16.6%, 19.5% and 24.8%, respectively. This work provided a new strategy of using CDs to mediate the coupling of microalgal bioresources production and toxic wastewater purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microalgae
Amoxicillin
Photosynthesis
Bioresource conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AMO and analysis of its degradation products
AMO was better removed and detoxification
Nitrogen and phosphorous was less remained

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chlorella growth profiles (a) and photosynthetic activity of photosystem II (represented by Fv/Fm) at different stages (b) under 3 mg/L AMO stress. CD-0 was the control (without CDs); CD-1 and CD-5 correspond to 1 and 5 mg/L CDs, respectively, supplied during cultivation. Error bars correspond to standard deviations (n ≥ 3). Bars marked with an asterisk (*) indicate treatments that were significantly different from the control within the same time point (P < 0.05).

Effects of CDs on AMO removal efficiencies (a) and normalized removal rates (mg-AMO/d/cell). CD (no Chlorella) was without Chlorella; AMO photolysis was without Chlorella and CDs. (b). Error bars represent the standard deviations of triplicate samples. Bars marked with an asterisk (*) indicate significant differences relative to the control within each time period (P < 0.05).

Inhibition of the AMO intermediates on S. aureus illuminance. Error bars represent the standard deviations of samples (six copies). Bars marked with an asterisk (*) correspond to treatments that are significantly different from the control at the same time point (P < 0.05).
Effect of CDs on the removal of nitrogen and phosphorus by Chlorella: (a) TN; (b) PO4 3−. Error bars represent standard deviations of triplicate samples.

The content and production of high-value bioresources by Chlorella under the stimulation of CDs. Pigments (a and b), proteins, carbohydrates and lipids (c and d). Pigment content was measured versus cultivation time; other comparisons (including pigment production) were made at day 18. Error bars represent the standard deviations of triplicate samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots enhance the recovery of microalgae bioresources from wastewater containing amoxicillin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Lab for Water Pollution Control and Resources Recovery of Jilin Province, School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Lab for Water Pollution Control and Resources Recovery of Jilin Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Lab for Water Pollution Control and Resources Recovery of Jilin Province, School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Taigang Engineering Technology Co. Ltd., Taiyuan 030000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Taigang Engineering Technology Co. Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Taigang Engineering Technology Co. Ltd., Taiyuan 030000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19 A Yuquan Road, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19 A Yuquan Road, Beijing 100049, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The cultivation of microalgae using wastewater could bring some major economic benefits; however, the toxics in wastewater typically lead to a reduction in bioresource production. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) could enhance the photosynthetic activity of Chlorella under antibiotic stress because they might optimize photoluminescence by red-shifting incident light. Adding of 1 mg/L CDs increased the specific growth rate of Chlorella by 36.0% (day 8–13) and 52.7% (day 14–18) and significantly increased photosystems II activity. This treatment also increased amoxicillin removal by 18.6%. Thus, the toxicity of residuals was significantly eliminated (P < 0.05). The removal of nitrogen and phosphorous was increased by 14.6% and 9.9%, respectively. The production of pigments, lipids and proteins was increased by 16.6%, 19.5% and 24.8%, respectively. This work provided a new strategy of using CDs to mediate the coupling of microalgal bioresources production and toxic wastewater purification.

ce:section.ce:para: Microalgae are an incredibly sustainable biomass feedstock that could replace fossil fuels in the future (Liao et al., 2016). The recovery of microalgae bioresources from wastewater could bring some major economic benefits, as this process could achieve both resource conversion and wastewater purification simultaneously (Hussain et al., 2020). Diverse bioactive compounds can be synthesized by microalgae from wastewater, including lipids, peptides or amino acids, antioxidants, polysaccharides and pigments (Zhuang et al., 2020). At the same time, organic carbon and nutrient (i.e., nitrogen and phosphorus) concentrations in wastewater can be decreased by more than 98%, thus minimizing ecological risks (Grandclement et al., 2017).

One of the major challenges of cultivating microalgae using wastewater is that the toxics in wastewater inevitably lead to a decrease in biomass production and bioresource synthesis. Various harmful and persistent organics have been identified in wastewater, including pharmaceuticals and personal care products, pesticides and hormones; antibiotics are one of the most widespread emerging contaminants (Zhu et al., 2020). Microalgae-based antibiotic removal highly depends on the antibiotic concentrations and characteristics. Specifically, β-lactams, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, sulfonamides, tetracyclines classes of antibiotics were most frequently detected in wastewater (Anjali and Shanthakumar, 2019) at μg/L and even mg/L levels (Villar-Navarro et al., 2018). It is worth to concern that the antibiotic inhibition on algae-based antibiotic removal and resources transformation processes.

The main mechanism by which antibiotics damage green algae is by disturbing photosynthesis (Yu et al., 2017). Tetracyclines, penicillin, β-lactam and macrolide antibiotics all significantly inhibit the growth and physiological processes of algae by disturbing primary photochemistry, electron transport, carbon assimilation and photophosphorylation (Chai et al., 2021). For example, amoxicillin (AMO), a β-lactam antibiotic, reduced the growth of A. cylindrica by 25.6% at low concentration (250 μg/L) (Zhong et al., 2021); it also caused the chlorophyll a content of C. pyrenoidosa to decrease by 79.92% over a relatively short timeframe (24 h) (Du et al., 2018). Specifically, AMO interfered with the photosynthesis of microalgae by blocking the electron transport chain of photosystem II (PSII) (Wang et al., 2019), which led to a significant reduction in biomass and photosynthetic metabolism.

Finding ways to improve photosynthetic activity might help circumvent the negative effects of antibiotics. Fluorescent materials could help optimize sunlight illumination. Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials that have attracted wide attention because of their ultrasmall size (<10 nm), high biocompatibility, photoluminescent properties, low cost and low toxicity (Li et al., 2020). These types of nanoparticles are often used as a spectral conversion material to extend the quantity of light exposed to microalgae. The synthesized CDs using a simple procedure with citric acid and formamide as precursors and found that they could adsorb light at 500–600 nm and emit red light at 580–700 nm. The CDs adsorbed on Chlorella regularis by bridging with extracellular polysaccharides and proteins and then provided sustainable and improved irradiation to cells; as a result, the relative expression of photosynthetic functional enzymes and cell growth increased by 20–70% and ~ 15%, respectively (Xue et al., 2020). The CDs transferred energy to the chloroplast and accelerated the electron transfer in PSII, suggesting that CDs could enhance the defensive capabilities of microalgae growing in wastewater containing antibiotics.

Here, the effects of CDs on microalgae biomass synthesis was characterized, antibiotics degradation and bioresource production to clarify how CDs contribute to enhancing microalgal bioresource production in antibiotic-containing wastewater. Thus, to simulate real wastewater compositions was more concerned. Specifically, conducting experiments in which the microalga Chlorella regularis was cultured in wastewater containing 3 mg/L AMO and characterized Chlorella growth profiles, AMO removal, degradation pathways, toxicity elimination and bioresource production. This work provided a new strategy on that CDs can mediate the coupling of advanced wastewater treatment and resource harvesting with microalgae.

Chlorella regularis (FACHB-729) was purchased from the Freshwater Algae Culture Collection Center of the Institute of Hydrobiology (Wuhan, China). The method of strain purification and pretreatment was based on a previously described study (Zhou et al., 2017).
CDs were prepared using citric acid and formamide as precursors as described in a previous study (Xue et al., 2020).

Chlorella was cultivated using simulated wastewater in 250-mL flasks (150 mL working volume). The ratio of nitrogen to phosphorus of simulated wastewater was 9:1, based on either the characteristics of wastewater or the nutritional requirements for microalgal metabolisms (Chao et al., 2019) and consisted of (mg/L) 273 NaNO3, 40 K2HPO4·3H2O, 36 CaCl2·2H2O, 6 Citric acid instead, 6 Ferric ammonium citrate, 1 EDTA and 20 Na2CO3, as well as 1.0 mL/L of a trace element solution. Chlorella cells that cultured for 12 days (late logarithmic phase) were inoculated into the simulated wastewater, at an initial cell density of 1.0 × 106 cells/mL and illuminated at 4000 lx using a light incubator (IS-6L, Crystal, America), which were cultivated at 25 °C with a photoperiod of 16 h light/8h dark. Three mg/L AMO (98%, C16H19N3O5S, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was added to the simulated wastewater based on the typical AMO residue concentrations found in the real wastewater. (Zhang et al., 2020).

To evaluate the effects of CDs concentrations on AMO removal and microalgal bioresources recovery, 0, 1, and 5 mg/L nanoparticles were added to the cultures every 6 days. They were referred to CD-0, CD-1, and CD-5 hereafter. CDs (in absence of microalgae) and AMO photolysis (in absence of microalgae and CD) were assumed as control protocol. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AMO and analysis of its degradation products
ce:section.ce:para: AMO concentrations were determined using an ultraperformance liquid chromatographer (UPLC, Waters, USA) equipped with a BEH C18 column (2.1 × 100 mm, 10 μm). The mobile phase consisted of 6% methanol and 94% ultra-pure water with formic acid (0.1%, v/v). Other detection conditions were as follows: column temperature 40 ℃, flow rate 0.2 mL/min, injection volume 10 μL and wavelength of the ultraviolet (UV) detector 254 nm. Samples were filtered through 0.22-μm microporous membrane (Jinlong, China) filters prior to analysis.

AMO intermediates were analyzed using an UPLC/MS system (Waters UPLC Acquity, Quattro Premier XE, USA) equipped with an ACQUITY UPLC BEH C18 column (100 × 2.1 mm, 2.6 μm). The mobile phase consisted of 0.1% formic acid and methanol, which was used for elution with a gradient changing from 95% to 5% (v/v); the flow rate was 0.25 mL/min. The column temperature was set at 40 °C. MS was performed using an ESI in both the negative and positive ion modes under the following conditions: source temperature 350 °C, heater temperature 320 °C and ion spray voltage −3.0 kV (negative mode)/3.2 kV (positive mode). The scanning mass ranged from 50 to 750 m/z at a resolution of 6 × 104. Data acquisition and processing were conducted using the Masslynx V4.1 software (Waters, USA) with the Qual and Quan browser.

The toxicity of AMO transformation products was evaluated using the gram-negative Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 27853 (Zhang et al., 2020). The samples were filtered through 0.22-μm microporous membrane filters (Jinlong, China) before testing. Next, 7 × 108 cells/mL S. aureus cells were incubated in 30-mL samples for 4 h at 37 °C with 150 rpm agitation. Finally, cells were counted using the standard colony-forming count method (Pereira et al., 2010). The inhibition rate is the ratio of the difference between the number of colonies in the experimental group (CD-0, CD-1 and CD-5) and the blank group (no antibiotics) according to Eq. (1)
(1)
Inhibition Rate
IR ,
%
=
C - B
/ C
×
1
00 %
where B and C are the colonies in the experimental group and the blank group, respectively. Six parallel samples were measured.

Maximum quantum yield (Fv/Fm) was used to evaluate the photosynthetic activity of PSII, which is the maximal PSII photochemical efficiency after a period of dark adaptation (Ralph et al., 2007). Using a chlorophyll fluorometer (AP110-C, AquaPen, Brno, Czech Republic) to measure the maximum fluorescence (Fm) and the minimum fluorescence (F0) of cells via irradiation with saturated pulsed light. Fv is the difference between Fm and F0. The maximum quantum yield was calculated using the equation Fv/Fm = (Fm − F0)/Fm.

Pigments, carbohydrates, proteins and lipids were measured as described in a previous study (Xue et al., 2020). The supernatant was collected to measure the absorbance of the pigments using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2800A, UNICO Co. Ltd., China) at 470, 652, 665 and 750 nm. Optical density (OD) at 470, 652, and 665 nm was used for chlorophyll and carotenoid determination, where OD at 750 nm was used as a turbidity correction factor. Values of OD at 750 nm were subtracted from each of the pigment OD values of the other wavelengths before using them in the equations below (Ji et al., 2014):
chlorophyll a
C a
,
mg
/ L
=
16
. 82 ×
OD 665
- 9 . 28 ×
OD 652
chlorophyll b
C b
,
mg
/ L
=
36
. 92 ×
OD 652
- 16 . 54 ×
OD 665
carotenoid
mg / L
=
1 000 ×
OD
47 0
- 1 . 19 ×
C a
- 95 . 15 ×
C b
/ 225
where OD470, OD652 and OD665 are the absorbances of the supernatant at 470, 652 and 665 nm, respectively.
The cell density-normalized pigment content and pigment production were determined by the ratio of pigment content and cell density.

Before intracellular carbohydrate and protein measurement, 10 mg algal powder was suspended in 3 mL ethanol (80%) and incubated at 68 ℃ for 15 min. Then, the suspension was centrifuged at 13,500 rpm, to retain supernatant A. The precipitate was added to 1.5 mL perchloric acid (30%) and mixed for 15 min, and supernatant B was obtained.

To measure carbohydrate, supernatants A and B were combined, and then anthrone-sulfuric acid method was employed (Zheng et al, 2017). To measure protein, 2 mL of 2 mol/L NaOH was added to above deposit and incubated at 100 ℃ for 10 min. The protein in this supernatant was determined using the method of Bradford (Bradford, 1976). The corresponding content and production were determined by g-component/g-biomass and g-component/L-culture, respectively.

Three mL chloroform/methanol (2/1, v/v) was added for lipid extraction of 30 mg algal powder, and they were mixed on a shaker (Mix-100, Allsheng, China) in the dark for 10 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 1,000 rpm to obtain supernatant, and then 1.5 mL of the above-described extraction reagent was added, followed by sonication at 40 kHz for 30 min. Samples were centrifuged again and then methanol and 0.1% NaCl solution were added to make a final ratio of methanol/chloroform/water of 1/1/0.9. Finally, the bottom layer was collected and dried at 40 ℃ using a nitrogen evaporator (KD2000, Allsheng, China) (Bligh and Dyer, 1959).

Total nitrogen (TN) in the culture was determined using a TOC meter (Multi N/C 3100, Analytik Jena, GER), and phosphate was determined by ammonium molybdate spectrophotometry (APHA, 1998). All the samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane before measurement.

To identify the effects of CDs on microalgae growth, AMO removal efficiency, rate, photosynthesis and intracellular components, comparing differences between CD treatments and controls. Statistical significance was analyzed with independent-sample t-tests. The significance threshold was set to P < 0.05, and all analyses were conducted using the SPSS software package (IBM SPSS statistic 20.0).

The average particle size of the synthetic CDs was 2.57 nm, with adsorption spectra peaks at 350 nm and 520 nm (Xue et al., 2020). When the prepared CDs were excited by simulated sunlight (LED), the emission peaks appeared red-shifted with a strong peak spanning 580–700 nm. As expected, this peak was within the range of the light absorption wavelength required for Chlorella photosynthesis (600–700 nm). Applications of these nanoparticles at the concentrations used (≤5 mg/L) were not toxic to Chlorella cells.

Chlorella cell density increased by 11.5% when 1 mg/L CDs (CD-1) was applied under 3 mg/L AMO stress (Fig. 1 a). The maximum cell density reached 4.72 × 107 cell/mL on day 18. Cell growth decreased after day 14, although nitrogen and phosphorus are not completely consumed, however, light energy transformation decreased due to the continuous cell density increment so that the cell grew slowly by then (Raeisossadati et al., 2018). Consequently, the specific growth rate was 36.0% greater for CD-1 (0.018 d-1) than the control lacking CDs (CD-0, 0.013 d-1) at the logarithmic phase (day 8–13) and further increased by 52.7% from day 14 to 18. This indicates that CDs provide illumination, support microalgae photosynthesis and ensure efficient light energy transformation at high cell density. For green microalgae, chlorophyll a and b are the major light-harvesting pigments, which have dual-absorbance ranges of blue (450–480 nm) and red (650–700 nm) light. Thus, such upconversion capability of CDs could improve the photosynthetic efficiency and microalgae growth (Ooms, et al, 2016).

However, 5 mg/L CDs (CD-5) had a negligible effect on Chlorella growth, probably because the excessive amount of transformed red light induced photic damage (Xue et al., 2020). Excess CDs thus led to the production of reactive oxygen species, which resulted in oxidative damage (Xiao et al., 2016).

CDs could also alleviate AMO inhibition on Chlorella photosynthesis by accelerating electron transfer in PSII, which is the primary and most sensitive site to inhibition by β-lactam antibiotics (Fig. 1b). Fv/Fm values were generally significantly increased for CD-1 (P < 0.05), indicating that the photosynthetic activity of microalgae was improved; this pattern was consistent with the cell growth results described above. CDs enhanced the efficiency of microalgae photosynthesis according to the above Fv/Fm activity and chlorophyll results. Then, their abilities to improve the AMO tolerance and detoxification capability of Chlorella was tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AMO was better removed and detoxification
ce:section.ce:para: AMO was 83% removed by Chlorella (CD-0) (Fig. 2 a), a non-target eukaryotic microorganism of antibiotics (Xiong et al., 2017). CDs had a negligible effect on AMO removal. With the addition of 1 mg/L CDs (CD-1), AMO removal increased by ~ 18.6%, and the efficiency reached ~ 99.3% at day 13. In addition, the increase in the cell density-normalized AMO removal rates was highly significant (P < 0.01) for CD-1, and the increase reached 54.3% during day 4–6. This indicated that CDs optimize the spectral properties and it promoted microalgal hydrolase expression, which contributed to the β-lactam antibiotic to biocatalyze β-lactam structure damage further biodegradation of antibiotics (Guo et al., 2016). CD-5 had a slight (but not significant) inhibitory effect on AMO removal.

AMO removal does not necessarily indicate that toxicity has been eliminated given that its intermediates still have adverse biological effects (Litskas et al., 2018). Thus, S. aureus inhibition tests were conducted to evaluate the toxicity of the degradation products (Fig. 3 ). The inhibition rate of AMO (0 d) was as high as 88%, but it decreased by ~43% after 4 days of degradation. CD-1 had the lowest inhibition rate (~25%), indicating that AMO was more completely decomposed. In sum, 1 mg/L CDs significantly increased AMO removal, and the toxicity of the products was also mostly eliminated. However, the inhibition rates of CD-5 and CD-0 did not significantly differ.

The degradation pathways of AMO are likely altered in the presence of CDs given that the dominant intermediates differed from the control. CDs led to the generation of smaller molecules; the products C15H18N2O3S (MW 306) and C8H12N2O3S, (MW 216) were not detected at day 12. Most importantly, CDs led to the opening of the β-lactam ring and the breaking of the amidase bond, indicating that nearly all AMO had been removed (Yu et al., 2017). However, there were several products with potential antibiotic activity that remained in CD-0, such as those with MWs of 216, 306 and 171. The structures and spectra of major degradation products are shown in supplementary material. These results explain the elimination of the toxicity of the intermediates for CD-1 observed in Fig. 3.

Enhancement of the photosynthesis of green algae can lead to the activation of enzymes that degrade β-lactam antibiotics, including oxidoreductases, reductases, transferases and hydrolases (Zhang et al., 2020). For AMO, hydrolases can induce hydrolysis reactions that open the β-lactam ring (Crofts et al., 2018). Oxidoreductases and reductase enzymes, such as peroxidase, dehydrogenase, oxidase and reductase, are well-known non-specific enzymes that contributed to the breaking of the amidase bond and led to more complete decomposition (Du et al., 2018). Thus, the improved illumination caused by CDs greatly contributed to the decomposition of AMO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen and phosphorous was less remained

ce:section.ce:para: Microalgae are efficient biological factories capable of taking waste in the form of CO2 and converting it into high-value bioresources (Ramanna et al., 2017). Photosynthetic pigments accumulated gradually during the first 8 days because of photosynthetic carbon assimilation and then slightly decreased (Fig. 5 a), likely because of the use of carbon in intracellular starch and lipid synthesis (Zhu et al., 2015). In CD-1, the total pigment content increased by 5.6%, and the chlorophyll a content increased by 6.7%.

Microalgae are currently being promoted as an ideal third generation biofuel feedstock; the conversion efficiency was determined by lipid production (Chu et al., 2020). The lipid content at day 18 was significantly increased (P < 0.05) compared with CD-0 (Fig. 5c-d), and this led to a 7.7% decrease in the intracellular carbohydrate content. The difference between available energy reserves (based on carbohydrates, proteins and lipids) and energy consumption reflects the overall condition of microalgae (Aderemi et al., 2018). An increase in lipid accumulation and the consumption of intracellular carbohydrates are typical metabolic indicators of environmental stress (e.g., light, salinity and nitrogen deficiency) (Zhang et al., 2017). CDs improved the photosynthesis of Chlorella, which in turn improved the carbon sequestration. Previous studies have shown that red light can induce a high lipid content in green algae (Aburai et al., 2020) and significantly increase the content of polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFA) (Fabian et al., 2014).

As mentioned above, CDs optimized the light emission wavelength, which improved the carbon conversion efficiency and promoted biomass photosynthesis (Ruangsomboon, 2011). The sum of extracted intracellular proteins, carbohydrates, and lipid was about 65%. The other parts of the biomass include residual ash (such as cell wall), extracellular polymeric substance (EPS), and organelles (Gonzalez et al., 2020). Moreover, extraction efficiencies of the intracellular components affect their ratio possessed in biomass (Zhang et al., 2018).

Overall, pigment, lipid and protein production were all significantly increased when CDs were present (Fig. 5). The pigment production for CD-1 reached 57.96 mg/L, which was 16.6% greater than that of CD-0. Although the contents of the total pigment were not dramatically increased, CDs favored to transfer light energy to the reaction center of photosystems to improve biomass production. As a result, intracellular bioresources (i.e., pigments and chlorophyll a) production finally improved. Microalgal lipid and protein production for CD-1 reached 501.83 mg/L and 142.60 mg/L, respectively, which were 19.5% and 24.8% higher than those for CD-0. CD-5 had little effect on changes in the intracellular components of microalgae. Thus, the optimal concentration of CDs enhanced the production of high-value bioresources by green microalgae.

In this study, for the negative effects of amoxicillin stress, the light wavelengths were shifted by CDs to the spectra that are precisely suitable for microalgae photosynthesis. The CDs led to a 36.0% (day 8–13) and 52.7% (day 14–18) increase in specific growth rate of Chlorella. The 1 mg/L CDs increased the AMO removal by 18.6% and damaged the β-lactam ring, which thereby eliminated the toxicity of the residual degradation products. The removal of TN and PO4 3- increased by 14.6% and 9.9%, respectively. Finally, the production of pigments, lipids and proteins was increased by 16.6%, 19.5% and 24.8%, respectively.

Zhenhao Zhao: Investigation, Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing -original draft, Formal analysis. Ren Xue: Investigation, Data curation. Liang Fu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Congli Chen: Formal analysis. Fabrice Ndayisenga: . Dandan Zhou: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52070036, U20A20322, 52070039), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2412018ZD042, 2412020FZ014) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2021.125258. The carbon dots of UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra, toxicity analysis of carbon dots, and mass spectra of AMO and intermediates eluted at different times.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Aburai et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) on lipid production of the aerial microalga Coccomyxa sp. KGU-D001 under liquid- and aerial-phase conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aburai, N., Kunishima, R., Iijima, F., Fujii, K., 2020. Effects of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) on lipid production of the aerial microalga Coccomyxa sp. KGU-D001 under liquid- and aerial-phase conditions. Journal of Biotechnology, 323. 10.1016/j.jbiotec.2020.09.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidative stress responses and cellular energy allocation changes in microalgae following exposure to widely used human antibiotics

Oxidative stress responses and cellular energy allocation changes in microalgae following exposure to widely used human antibiotics. Aquatic Toxicol., 203, 130-139. 10.1016/j.aquatox.2018.08.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insights on the current status of occurrence and removal of antibiotics in wastewater by advanced oxidation processes

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chai et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifaceted roles of microalgae in the application of wastewater biotreatment: a review
